prompt_used: Ultra-high-end product photography of a structured backpack in matte Core Black, shot at a precise 30-degree 3/4 angle on a seamless dark navy (#0B1626) background. The pack is 22L — its volume is evident from its proportions: taller than it is wide, structured, not slouching. The front face of the pack prominently features a horizontal attachment rail system — a stainless steel and nylon channel running across the front panel, clearly designed for accessory modules to snap on. This rail is the hero detail of the shot: it should be sharp, precise, and distinguishable from the pack body. The shell material is 600D ballistic nylon: visible crosshatch weave pattern under lighting, matte black with slight surface variation, not shiny or smooth. YKK zipper pulls along the main compartment and front pocket are matte black metal, small and precise. The lighting setup: a single main key light from upper-left at 45 degrees creates sharp material texture on the nylon surface. A subtle rim light from the right edge creates body definition. A third low-powered light from behind and below emits a soft electric mint (#3FFFAB) glow — it catches the bottom edge of the pack and the module rail, creating a ghost of green-blue luminescence. This glow is not a halo, not saturated — it is a whisper of colour that suggests technology and craft. Camera focal length equivalent: 85-100mm. Ultra-shallow depth of field with the front face in perfect focus, background fading into pure navy darkness. No people, no hands, no props, no text, no watermarks. Every surface detail sharp enough to count stitches.

section: Modular System Explainer — product concept diagram
prompt_used: A meticulously composed product concept flat-lay photograph on a seamless dark navy (#0B1626) surface. The TrailVolt Core Pack in Core Black is centred in the frame — photographed front-facing with the module attachment rail clearly visible across the front face. The pack is elevated slightly or arranged to cast a clean shadow. Around the central pack, arranged in a radial pattern at equal distances, are all 8 snap-on modules — each physically separated from the pack by a deliberate gap of approximately 4-6cm in the composition, as if hovering just before attachment. The 8 modules, each distinct in shape and function: (1) a slim rectangular chest phone holder pouch with magnetic clasp, (2) a flat multi-pocket tech organizer with cable loops visible, (3) a brick-shaped power bank unit with USB ports visible on one edge, (4) a ventilated shoe bag — slightly taller with mesh panel, (5) a compression clothing cube — flatter and wider, (6) a slim document sleeve — flat and rectangular with RFID-blocking visible, (7) a side-swing bottle holder — cylindrical sleeve with retractable arm, (8) a folded rain cover in its storage pouch. Each module is premium product photography quality: sharp, matte black or dark grey finish, YKK zipper pulls where applicable. Thin electric mint (#3FFFAB) lines radiate from the central pack's attachment rail toward each module — these are light-traced connection paths, not graphic arrows or overlays. They look like light threads or photographic light painting, not digital annotations. Studio lighting on all objects simultaneously — no shadows that block module detail. Clean dark navy background throughout. No text, no labels, no arrows, no watermarks.

A candid editorial-style portrait of a man in his early-to-mid 30s of Eastern European appearance — slim build, dark hair, focused expression — working in a workshop-studio environment. He is not looking at the camera. He is mid-task: either examining a section of fabric on a backpack prototype, holding a hardware component (a buckle or zipper pull) up to the light, or marking fabric with chalk. The setting: a creative workshop or design studio in Dublin — raw concrete or exposed-brick wall behind him, a long workbench with materials laid out: a partially assembled backpack prototype in black, fabric swatches in Cordura and ripstop nylon, small hardware samples (Duraflex buckles, YKK zipper pulls), a MacBook open on one side showing what appears to be a technical CAD drawing or material spec sheet. The lighting is natural — a large window to one side casts soft directional daylight. The image feels like it was taken by a journalist who was in the room: slightly imperfect framing, authentic ambient environment, no studio setup. The man's clothing: smart casual — a quality navy or dark grey sweatshirt, no brand logos visible. His posture is engaged and intent — this is someone absorbed in a problem, not performing for a camera. The overall aesthetic: a Monocle magazine interview portrait meets a Gear Patrol behind-the-scenes visit.

section: Everyday Line — Core Black product card (Best Seller)
prompt_used: Clean, precise studio product photography of the TrailVolt Everyday 22L pack in Core Black. The pack is photographed front-facing or at a slight 3/4 angle — enough to show both the front face and one side panel. Background: dark navy (#0B1626) to very dark charcoal gradient, shot on seamless paper. The pack form: structured 22L volume — not flat, not oversized. The top handle is visible and taut. The shoulder straps emerge from the top and are partially visible at the frame edges. The front face: the module attachment rail is the primary foreground detail — a horizontal system of connection points running across the front panel, matte stainless or dark metal. Above it, the rapid-access front organiser zip sits tight against the panel. The main compartment wrap-around zip runs the full body perimeter. The material: 600D ballistic nylon in matte Core Black — the weave is a visible crosshatch pattern, approximately 2mm spacing, slightly rough under the lighting. Not shiny, not smooth, not rubber-like. The YKK zipper pulls: matte gunmetal or matte black, small and precise, oval loop profile. Lighting: key light from upper-right at 40 degrees creates texture relief across the nylon surface; a subtle fill from the left prevents shadow crushing; a thin rim light from behind creates pack silhouette separation from the background. No electric mint in this shot — pure product commercial photography. No people, no hands, no props, no text. Ultra-sharp, 8K quality.

section: Three Lines section — Trail line card
prompt_used: A cinematic outdoor lifestyle photograph shot in Irish highland terrain — specifically the Wicklow Mountains or Wild Atlantic Way coastal hills: rounded green ridgelines, overcast sky with breaks of golden-hour light at the horizon, misty valley below, rough boggy trail surface. A single hiker — gender-neutral to male, early 30s, capable and focused — is mid-stride on the trail moving away from or across the camera, not toward it. They are not posed, not resting, not looking back. The TrailVolt Trail 28L in Forest Green (#2D5016) is on their back. The pack's Forest Green colour is clearly legible — not black, not dark olive, but a deep natural green that reads as intentional outdoor colourway against the Irish terrain. The pack is loaded: compression side straps are pulled taut suggesting full contents, the shoulder harness is visibly engaged and distributing load. On the left or right side panel of the pack, a Bottle Holder Module holds a metal water bottle — the bottle's top is visible extending slightly above the holder. The Cordura 500D shell texture catches the side light — slightly wet surface suggesting Irish weather has been present, DWR-treated so water beads faintly visible. The camera angle: from behind or slightly to the side of the hiker — a tracking shot or observational frame. Focal length equivalent: 50-85mm. The landscape dominates the background — mist, green hills, dramatic sky — but the pack is the clear subject, filling approximately 25-35% of the frame. Golden-hour rim light from behind the hiker creates a subtle silhouette separation. The overall mood: earned, elemental, real. Not a photoshoot — a photograph taken on an actual hike.
